coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent N,S-carbon dots from bamboo leaf and the interaction with nitrophenol compounds
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xiupei
Wang, Dan
Luo, Na
Feng, Min
Peng, Xiaohui
Liao, Xiangjun
coredata.dc:description: A simple and green approach for the synthesis of photoluminescent N,S-carbon dots (N,S-CDs) has been proposed using a single natural source precursor (bamboo leaf) as raw materials. The as-synthesized N,S-CDs exhibited a highly stable, excitation wavelength-dependent emission, excellent photobleaching, alkali, and salt tolerance. Here, the mechanism of N,S-CDs luminescence was studied via the UV–vis absorption spectrum and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Based on the quenching properties of nitrophenol compounds on the fluorescence of N,S-CDs, the interaction between N,S-CDs and nitrophenol compounds was investigated on detail in aqueous solution. More importantly, the study on photophysical properties of the N,S-CDs may provide the basis for the development of the N,S-CDs for the fluorescent probe of nitrophenol compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,S-carbon dots
Nitrophenols
Fluorescence quenching
Quenching mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of Förster radius, energy transfer efficiency, and donor-acceptor distance
Structure and morphology of as-prepared N,S-CD
Analysis of fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism
Analysis of the charge transfer mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology characterization of N,S-CDs: (A) TEM image of N,S-CDs (inset: HRTEM image of N,S-CDs); (B) particle size distribution of N,S-CDs.
Chemical structure of N,S-CDs: (A) XPS spectra; (B) S2p spectra; (C) C1s spectra; (D) N1s spectra; (E) O1s spectra; (F) FTIR spectra of N,S-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption (left) and fluorescence emission (middle and right) spectra of N,S-CDs (the inset pattern is photographs under visible light and UV-light of N,S-CDs); (B) excitation spectra of N,S-CDs with different concentrations.
The quenching fluorescence spectra of the system by 2,4,6-TNP.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs with 100 μM 2/3/4-NP (the inset pattern is photographs under visible light (up) and UV-light (down) of the N,S-CDs, ABCD are belong to N,S-CDs, N,S-CDs + 2-NP, N,S-CDs + 3-NP, 05N,S-CDs + 4-NP); (B) effects of different nitrophenol and other aromatic organic compounds on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs.
The overlapping between the UV–vis absorption spectra of 2,4-TNP/2,4,6-DNP and the fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs.
Fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs with 50 μM 4-NP/2,4-DNP/2,4,6-TNP.
(A) Cyclic voltammogram of the N,S-CDs in 0.1 M Bu4NPF6/DMF solution at 100 mV/s; (B) optical band gap of N,S-CDs obtained from the UV–vis absorption spectrum.
Scheme for N,S-carbon dots preparation strategy and fluorescence quenching of them by nitrophenol compounds.
Fluorescence quenching parameter of the solution systems of N,S-CDs and nitrophenol compounds at 25 °C.
Theoretical calculated energy levels of some nitroaromatics N,S-CDs and nitrobenzene phenolic compounds.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent N,S-carbon dots from bamboo leaf and the interaction with nitrophenol compounds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Exposure and Biomonitoring Division, Health Canada, 50 Colombine Driveway, Ottawa K1A 0K9, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Exposure and Biomonitoring Division, Health Canada, 50 Colombine Driveway, Ottawa K1A 0K9, Canada

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and green approach for the synthesis of photoluminescent N,S-carbon dots (N,S-CDs) has been proposed using a single natural source precursor (bamboo leaf) as raw materials. The as-synthesized N,S-CDs exhibited a highly stable, excitation wavelength-dependent emission, excellent photobleaching, alkali, and salt tolerance. Here, the mechanism of N,S-CDs luminescence was studied via the UV–vis absorption spectrum and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Based on the quenching properties of nitrophenol compounds on the fluorescence of N,S-CDs, the interaction between N,S-CDs and nitrophenol compounds was investigated on detail in aqueous solution. More importantly, the study on photophysical properties of the N,S-CDs may provide the basis for the development of the N,S-CDs for the fluorescent probe of nitrophenol compounds.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of carbon-based zero-dimensional fluorescent materials, which are composed of dispersed spherical carbon particles with extremely small size (below 10 nm), with superior properties of stability, eco-friendly, low toxicity, good water solubility [1,2], which have attracted more and more attention in recent years [3]. The initial property needed for the discovery and application of CDs is that they have photoluminescence properties. Generally speaking, CDs with good water solubility will emit bright fluorescence when exposed to light, and their optical stabilities are very good. Therefore, CDs are widely used as fluorescent probes to detect some pollutants [4–6]. In general, hydrothermal/calcination treatment is one of the most prevalent methods due to the surface modification is simple and controllable, and the morphology of CDs is homogeneous [7,8], corresponding to that there are so many ways to synthesize CDs including chemical ablation [9], electrochemical synthesis [10], ultrasonic [11], and microwave irradiation [12]. Therefore, hydrothermal/calcination treatment is a very worthwhile technology to use and develop. Besides, natural biomass materials are common carbon sources for preparing CDs and are often synthesized by this method. Rich in natural ingredients and chemical elements, orange juice [13], strawberry [14], pear juice [15], incorporating evergreen bamboo leaves are one of the preferred natural carbon sources.

Nitrophenols are contaminants with high toxic potential levels and intermediates of synthetic dyes, indicators, drugs, leather products, etc. [16]. Nitrophenols, as a pollutant, they can not only enter lakes and rivers, seep into soil and groundwater but also enter the body through breathing, skin contact, swallowing, etc., and stimulate the eyes, skin, digestion and respiratory system. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a highly sensitive and selective analytical method for the determination of nitrophenols [17–19].

Among the existing detection methods, fluorescent sensors have gained considerable attention due to their high sensitivity and simplicity. Nowadays, there are many fluorescent probes to detect nitrophenols by energy transfer, charge transfer or internal filtration, and so on. Due to the rapid development of CDs in the field of analysis and detection, and based on its fluorescence characteristics, it is often used as a probe to determine nitrophenol [20,21]. Although the structure and composition of CDs are easy to elucidate, the mechanism of detecting nitrophenols is still not completely clear. For example, some 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection methods were constructed based on the fluorescence quenching of different carbon dots [22–24]. However, there are many types of nitrophenol, and if these substances coexist with 2,4,6-trinitrophenol, what effect they have on the measurement results has not been discussed in detail in previous reports. Therefore, revealing the interaction mechanism of different nitrophenol compounds on carbon dots is of great significance for further understanding of the mutual interference between them when measuring nitrophenol compounds.

Recently years, our group has done a series of work on carbon point synthesis and sensing applications [25–29]. Based on the previous work, biomass-derived carbon dots (N,S-CDs) with excellent fluorescence stability obtained by an efficient one-step hydrothermal synthesis through calcining bamboo leaf. The as-synthesized N,S-CDs were characterized by TEM, XPS, FT-IR, UV–vis and FL, and the mechanism of fluorescence emission was investigated. By analyzing the fluorescent properties, we were surprised to find that the N,S-CDs are very stable in storage, resistant to photobleaching and various chemicals, including acids, bases, and salts. The systematic quenching behaviors of N,S-CDs were explored in this work. The mechanisms of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), internal charge transfer (ICT), and electron transfer (ET) presumably dominate the whole suppression process of N,S-CDs caused by nitrophenols. The results of this study may provide some basis for the mechanism of N,S-CDs as fluorescent probes for the determination of nitrophenol in samples.

Fresh bamboo leaves were collected from the green belt of our university and washed by ultrapure water before use. Quinine sulfate, 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), hydroquinone, resorcinol, and catechol were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corp. (Shanghai, China). 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (2,4,6-TNP), toluene, nitrobenzene (NB), Benzene, m-dinitrobenzene (m-DHB) phenol, xylene, and aniline were received from a domestic commercial supplier. All chemicals were analytical reagent grade and used without further purification.

The bamboo leaves were dried in the oven at 100 °C, pulverized by a pulverizer, and sieved through a 60 sieve. The sample was sealed at room temperature. 2.0 g of the powder of bamboo leaves was placed in a quartz crucible inside a muffle furnace. The temperature was increased to 300 °C within 150 min, and the powder was annealed at 300 °C for 180 min. After being cooled to ambient temperature naturally, the black product was obtained. 1.0 g of the black powder and 10 mL ultrapure water were added into the beaker and sonicated for 30 min. The resulting mixture was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the impurity, and the filtrate was collected. Finally, the solid-state N,S-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying the filtrate and stored at 4 °C. In this article, the concentration of the solution of N,S-CDs used was 0.1 g/mL.

Morphology images of the transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution electron microscope (HRTEM) were obtained from a TECNAI G2 F20 electron microscope (FEI, USA) operating at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was taken on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI electron energy disperse spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with X-ray source Al (hν = 1486.6 eV), C1s (284.8 eV) was used to the calibration of binding energy. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded with a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA), and the test ranges 4000 cm−1–400 cm−1. Fluorescence spectra were investigated on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, Palo Alto, CA, USA). The fluorescence lifetime was estimated by an FLS980 transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd. UK). All ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were conducted with a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). Cyclic voltammograms (CV) were obtained from an MPI-E electrochemiluminescence analysis system (Ruimai, China) with a three-electrode system including a platinum wire electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode, and a glassy carbon electrode.

Quantum yield (φ) of as-prepared CDs was referred to as the previously reported method. Briefly, quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (φ 54% at 360 nm excitation) was used as a reference standard. The intensities of absorption spectra and integrated fluorescence of quinine sulfate and N,S-CDs were detected at the same conditions, and φ was calculated using the following equation [30,31]:
(1)
φ χ
=
φ std
I χ
A χ
A std
I
std
η χ
η std
2
where φ, A, I, and η are the quantum yield, the absorbance, the corrected emission intensity, and the refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The “std” and “x” are represented the reference standard and sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of Förster radius, energy transfer efficiency, and donor-acceptor distance
ce:section.ce:para:
The spectral overlap between the acceptor's absorption spectra and the donor emission spectrum implies a high probability for FRET to occur. According to Förster's theory, the Förster radius R0 is the distance between the two luminophores (donor and acceptor). The Förster radius was calculated according to the following equation [32]:
(2)
R 0
= 0.2108
κ 2
× ϕ ×
n R
− 4
× J
λ
1 6
where k 2 is the dipole orientation factor taken as equal to 2/3 for the case when a random donor-acceptor dipole orientation is assumed. The refractive index of the medium and the fluorescence quantum yield of the donor are represented by n and φ, and n is the refractive index of water at here is 1.36. Due to the high dilution of the sample, the influence of nanoparticle concentration on both the refractive index and the dipole orientation factor has been assumed to be marginal. The normalized spectral overlap J (λ) was calculated from the following equation,
(3)
J
λ
=
∫ 0 ∞
F
D
λ
ε
A
λ
λ 4
dλ
where F
D(λ) is the donor luminescence emission, ε
A(λ) the extinction coefficient of the acceptor, and λ the wavelength, respectively.

The luminescence emission decays were monitored for the donor N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of nitrophenol compounds, as the change in the donor decay time indicates successful FRET. The FRET efficiencies were calculated from amplitude-averaged decay times. The transfer efficiency E and the donor-acceptor distance r were calculated for RET from N,S-CDs using following equations,
(4)
E = 1 −
τ D
τ DA
=
1
1 +
r
R 0
6
where τDA and τD are decay times for a donor in the presence and absence of the acceptor. Noticeably, we used the standard Förster's theory considering energy transfer to be a consequence of interactions between two dipoles. We are aware of the fact that for multiple donors and acceptors transfer mechanism has not been fully understood, and the application of the classical Förster's theory may lead to certain calculation discrepancies.

Cyclic voltammograms of the N,S-CDs were obtained on an electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system, including a platinum wire electrode as the counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and a glassy carbon electrode as the working electrode. 0.1 M Bu4NPF6/acetonitrile solution was used as the electrolyte. The optical band gap of N,S-CDs was calculated from the UV–vis absorption spectra based on BaSO4. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the N,S-CDs were estimated according to the following formula [33]:
(5)
E HOMO
= − e
E ox
+ 4.4
(6)
E LUMO
= − e
E Red
+ 4.4
(7)
E HOMO
=
E LUMO
−
E g
where E
ox and E
Red are the onsets of oxidation and reduction potential for C-dots, respectively. Eg represents the optical band gap.

As is shown in Scheme 1 , N,S-carbon dots (N,S-CDs) could be synthesized by a simple calcination method using a single natural substance bamboo leaf as the carbon precursor. The obtained N,S-CDs exhibit high stability, excitation wavelength-dependent emission, excellent photobleaching, alkali, and salt tolerance. Of particular interest is the fact that different types of nitrophenol compounds have different quenching effects on the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs in aqueous solution. In addition, the quenching strength of 4-NP, 2,4-DNP, 2,4,6-TNP on the fluorescence of N,S-CDs is well correlated with their concentration. The application of as-prepared N,S-CDs provides a simple and green platform for the identification and highly sensitive detection of nitrophenol compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of as-prepared N,S-CD
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of N,S-CDs nanoparticles were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images in Fig. 1A indicated that the N,S-CDs nanoparticles were monodisperse uniform distribution in aqueous, which implied that the samples have good water solubility. A typical HRTEM of N,S-CDs nanoparticles was displayed in the inset of Fig. 1A, which revealed the amorphous nature of the as-prepared N,S-CDs [34]. The size-distribution of the N,S-CDs indicated that the particle size ranged from 4 to 22 nm, with an average diameter of about 11 nm presented in Fig. 1B.

To further investigate the surface composition and chemical structure, the XPS of N,S-CDs was recorded and presented in Fig. 2A–E. The XPS survey spectra (shown in Fig. 2A) revealed four characteristic absorption bands at 168.6 eV, 284.6 eV, 400 eV, and 531.6 eV [35], which were attributed to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, and the ratio of the four elements in N,S-CDs were to be 3.71% (S), 46.66% (C), 2.89% (N), 46.74% (O), respectively. It has been reported that N and S might change the electronic structure of CDs, thus regulating their fluorescence properties [36]. The presence of S2p and N1s band indicated that both S and N from the starting natural material were retained in the final N,S-CDs, which might enable them to tune the internal electronic structure of the N,S-CDs and the luminous properties via synergic effects. After deconvolution of the S2p band (Fig. 2B), the peaks in XPS at 167.6 eV, 168.3 eV, 169.2 eV could be assigned to the functional groups of -C-SOx (X = 2, 3, 4) [37]. The high-resolution scan of the C1S spectrum (Fig. 2C) could be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.5 eV, 285 eV, 286.2 eV, 288.3 eV, which were from C-C/C=C, C-S, C-N, C=O/C=N bands, respectively [38]. Such results were consistent with the FTIR results. The N 1s peaks at 399.5 and 400.4 eV shown in Fig. 2D indicated that nitrogen existed mostly in the form of C-N-C and N-(C)3 [14,33], respectively. The spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2E) exhibited two relative oxygen species of CO (531.0 eV) and CO (532.1 eV) [39]. To further confirm the existence of the functional groups in N,S-CDs, From the FTIR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 2F, the stretching vibrations of NH and OH were observed at 3432 cm−1 [40], indicating that the functional hydroxyl and amino groups were presented in the N,S-CDs, and might be responsible for the good solubility of N,S-CDs in water. The absorption bands at 1637 cm−1, 1384 cm−1, 1122 cm−1, 619 cm−1 suggested the stretching vibrations from functional groups of C=N, C-NH, C-O, C-S, respectively [41].

The optical properties of N,S-CDs were obtained by their UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The inset in Fig. 3 showed the spectra of the N,S-CDs obtained under visible (left), and UV light at 365 nm. Comparing with their strong blue-fluorescence at 365 nm, N,S-CDs were almost transparent in visible light. Additionally, their UV–vis spectrum indicated that N,S-CDs exhibited two UV–vis absorption peaks centered at approximately 240 nm and 310 nm, which could be attributed to the transition of π-π⁎ and n-π⁎ and corresponded to the fluorescence excitation peak [42]. The origin of fluorescence emission might come from quantum size effect [43], zig-zag sites [44], defect effect [45], and electronic conjugate structures [46]. The blue line and red line in Fig. 3 were corresponded to the maximum fluorescent excitation and fluorescent emission at 313 nm and 419 nm, respectively. Interestingly, a small peak at 238 nm was observed in the excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs, and its corresponding weak emission spectrum is about 419 nm. Hence, the blue-fluorescence of N,S-CDs may be made up of two parts: the n-π⁎ transition of surface defects (excitation at 313 nm) and the π-π⁎ transition of sp2 hybrid conjugated electronic structure (excitation at 238 nm) [47]. Since the emission spectrum obtained at the excitation at 313 nm is stronger and more stable in fluorescence intensity, the excitation wavelength at 313 nm was selected for the following studies.

The fluorescence emission spectrum of N,S-CDs was monitored at progressively increasing excitation wavelengths, and the results were shown in Fig. S1. It can be seen that a slight redshift was observed in the emission of N,S-CDs from 283 nm to 363 nm with increasing excitation wavelengths. The maximum fluorescence emission intensity was obtained when it was excited at 313 nm. This observation was in agreement with the report that the excitation wavelength is dependent on the emission wavelength and intensity [48]. With quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference, the fluorescence quantum yield of N,S-CDs was calculated to be 5.18% according to formula (1).

To further study the optical properties of N,S-CDs, We have investigated the effects of pH, high concentration of salts, common metal ions, and UV radiation time on the luminescence of N, S-CDs. The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs was stable at pH ranging from 4 to 11, and the fluorescence emission peak remained constant under different pH values tested (Fig. S2A), which indicated that the pH had no effects on the fluorescence spectrum of N,S-CDs. The reason may be from protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups [49] or surface charge density variation [32]. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs were also observed to be very stable even if the concentration of NaCl was as high as 1.0 M (Fig. S2B). The fluorescence intensity almost remained unchanged under continuous irradiation for 180 min (Fig. S2C), which indicated that N,S-CDs possessed good photostability. The synthesized carbon dots are often used to detect metal ions because of the complexation of metal ions with carbon dots [50]. Therefore, the stability of N,S-CDs on various metal ions was investigated and shown in Fig. S2D. The results suggested that no significant effect was observed of metal ions studied on the luminescence of N,S-CDs. Hence, the use of N,S-CDs in fluorescence imaging, and sensing may be feasible under physiological conditions.

Through our preliminary study, we looked for the target of the N,S-CDs through investigating a variety of reagents and drugs. It was found that the fluorescence of N,S-CDs could be effectively quenched by nitrophenols, whose physicochemical properties are slightly altered by the substituents on the aromatic ring [32]. Fig. 4 showed the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CD with 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP). When 0.1 mM TNP was added, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CD could be reduced by 80%. A similar phenomenon was observed in the addition of other nitrophenol compounds, such as 2-NP, 3-NP, and 4-NP. As shown in Fig. 5A, 100 μM of 2/3/4-NP could cause N,S-CDs showed different degrees of fluorescence quenching effect, respectively. The inset was photos of them taken under visible light, and 365 nm UV light, which was in contrast to their fluorescence spectrum corresponds perfectly, respectively. It can be seen that the N,S-CDs + 4-NP system has the largest quenching degree, the N,S-CDs + 2-NP system takes the second place, and the N,S-CDs + 3-NP quenching degree is relatively the smallest. The effects of different nitrophenol and other aromatic organic compounds on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs were shown in Fig. 5B. It indicated that no significant quenching behaviors occurred to other aromatic compounds of tests such as toluene, nitrobenzene (NB), benzene, m-dinitrobenzene (m-DHB) phenol, xylene, and aniline (Fig. 5).

The fluorescence quenching includes static and dynamic quenching [51]. The Stern-Volmer plot can distinguish the quenching mechanism. The Stern-Volmer quenching constant (K
SV ) for N,S-CDs quenched by nitrophenol compounds were calculated by Eq. (8):
(8)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
where F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensity observed of N,S-CDs in the presence and absence of nitrophenol compounds, respectively [Q] is the concentration of quencher (nitrophenol compounds), and Kq is the Apparent rate constant. The τ 0 is the average fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs in the absence of quencher, which is usually assumed to be 10−8 s. In the case of dynamic quenching, the quenching constants of bimolecular are generally 102– 103 L·mol−1 [52].

As can be seen from Fig. S3, a good linear relationship was observed in the Stern-Volmer plot of N,S-CDs quenched by nitrophenol compounds. The results were summarized in Table 1 . Compared with the dynamic quenching constants of bimolecular, the quenching constants of 2-NP and 3-NP are between 102 L·mol−1 and 103 L·mol−1, indicating that the quenching type of the system was dynamic quenching. Moreover, the measurement of fluorescence lifetime is an important means to understand and distinguish the mechanism of dynamic quenching and static quenching [53]. As seen from Table S1, the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs with 2-NP, 3-NP, and 4-NP were obviously decreased by deconvolution, proving the mechanism of dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Energy transfer is typically one of the mechanisms of fluorescence quenching with the one requirement of overlapping fluorescence emission from donor and energy absorption spectrum from the acceptor. Thus, we have studied and compared the spectral overlap of nitrophenol compounds. It can be seen from Fig. 6 that there is a significant overlap in the fluorescence emission of N,S-CDs and the energy absorption of 2,4,6-TNP. The donor-acceptor spectral overlap of other nitrophenol compounds was displayed in Fig. S4. The distance of Föster (R0) and the degree of overlap (J (λ)) can be estimated by Eqs. (2)–(4), and Table S2 lists the J (λ) and r values for nitrophenol compounds in this study. Another requirement for Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) quenching mechanism is between the receptor and the donor, which must be 10–100 Å [54]. The distance from our study was between 10 and 100 Å, suggesting that the interaction between nitrophenol compounds (energy acceptor) and N,S-CDs (energy donor) occurred to lead to fluorescence resonance energy transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the charge transfer mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: It is interesting to note that 4-NP, 2,4-DNP, and 2,4,6-TNP have the value of the quenching constant roughly 10 times as high as that for bimolecular (Table 1). This may indicate that the functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino in N,S-CDs might interact with the phenolic hydroxyl group of 4-NP, 2,4-DNP, and 2,4,6-TNP. Fig. 7 illustrated the fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs, and N,S-CDs with 4-NP, 2,4-DNP and 2,4,6-TNP, respectively. Redshifts in fluorescence spectra were observed with the increase of the quantity of quencher nitro groups. In the case of N,S-CDs an internal charge transfer (ICT) with the strong electron-withdrawing group from the compounds such as 4-NP, 2,4-DNP, and 2,4,6-TNP may occur to cause the fluorescence quenching. The redshift is observed obviously with the electron-withdrawing group possesses strengthened [55]. Based on the above phenomena, on the one hand, we can qualitatively determine whether 4-NP/2,4-DNP/2,4,6-TNP exists according to the emission spectrum is red-shifted. On the other hand, we can roughly quantify the total amount of these three nitrophenol compounds.

Another mechanism of fluorescence quenching is electron transfer. The quenching efficiency is proportional to the magnitude of the electron-withdrawing effect. The order of the magnitude of the electron-withdrawing effect in these three compounds is 2,4,6-TNP > 2,4-DNP > 4-NP [56]. An abnormal trend (2,4,6-TNP > 4-NP > 2,4-DNP) was observed in our study. In general, the main driving force for electron transfer is the energy gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of donor and acceptor. We calculated the energy levels of some nitrophenol compounds by density functional theory (DFT). The energy levels of LUMO and HOMO of N,S-CDs were estimated as 3.08 eV and −6.79 eV, respectively, according to Fig. 8 and the empirical formulae (5)–(7). Simultaneously, the LUMO and HOMO of nitrophenol compounds exhibited in Table 2 , indicating that electron transfer (ET) between N,S-CDs and 4-NP/2,4,6-TNP were possible.

In summary, biomass-derived carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized by the calcination method using fresh bamboo leaves as carbon source. They exhibited the potential for the selective detection of nitrophenol compounds because of their water stability, and electrostatic interactions with nitrophenols. The systematic quenching behaviors of N,S-CDs were elucidated from FRET, ICT, and ET. Experimental evidence implied that FRET was the main mechanism between N,S-CDs and nitrophenol compounds, and the synergistic effect might be from ICT and ET. This study provides innovative insights into the interaction mechanism between N, S-CDs and nitrophenol compounds. It will provide effective theoretical support for the construction of probes for detecting nitrophenol compounds based on N,S-CDs fluorescence quenching.

Xiupei Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Dan Wang: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Na Luo: Investigation, Software, Writing - original draft. Min Feng: Software, Visualization. Xiaohui Peng: Validation. Xiangjun Liao: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21777130, 21277109), the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province of China (19YYJC2801), the Meritocracy Research Funds of China West Normal University (463132) and the Fundamental Research Funds of China West Normal University (416390).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118462.
